era_context: The High Middle Ages; the Crusader States are engaged in ongoing conflict with Islamic powers in the Levant. The Byzantine Empire under Manuel I Komnenos maintains diplomatic and military engagement with both Crusaders and Islamic powers. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controls northern China; the Southern Song Dynasty rules the south. Japan is in the early phase of Taira clan dominance. Islamic civilization is at a high point of cultural and scientific achievement.

events.title: Nur ad-Din Zengi dies; his empire fragments
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi (Nūr ad-Dīn Maḥmūd ibn Zengi), the powerful Zengid ruler of Syria and Mesopotamia, dies. His death marks a turning point in the political history of the Levant. Nur ad-Din's empire, which had unified much of Syria and Mesopotamia under his rule, begins to fragment among his heirs and successors. This fragmentation creates an opportunity for Saladin to consolidate power in Egypt and eventually expand his influence into Syria. The death of Nur ad-Din is thus a crucial event that enables Saladin's rise to dominance in the Islamic world.
key_figures: As-Salih Ismail (Nur ad-Din's son, inherits Syria)

sources.name: Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin
Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of Syria

events.certainty_note: Islamic sources confirm Nur ad-Din's death in 1165 (Safar 560 AH). The event is well-attested and dated.
cross_references: Leads to 1166-003 (Saladin assumes command in Egypt)
Leads to 1187-001 (Saladin's conquest of the Levant)

events.title: Taira no Kiyomori defeats the Minamoto clan
events.description: Taira no Kiyomori defeats the Minamoto clan in a series of military engagements, consolidating Taira dominance over the imperial court and the military aristocracy. The Minamoto clan, traditional rivals of the Taira, are forced to retreat and lose influence at court. This victory marks a crucial step in the Taira clan's rise to dominance in Japan, which will last until their defeat in 1185.
key_figures: Minamoto no Yoshitomo (Minamoto clan leader, defeated)
events.certainty_note: Japanese sources attest to Taira-Minamoto conflicts in the 1160s, but the specific dating of individual engagements to 1165 is uncertain. The Heiji Rebellion (1159–1160) was a major Taira victory; subsequent conflicts in the 1160s are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Follows from 1159-001 (Heiji Rebellion, earlier Taira victory)

events.title: Amalric I of Jerusalem and Byzantine Empire form alliance
events.description: King Amalric I of Jerusalem and the Byzantine Emperor Manuel I Komnenos form a military and diplomatic alliance against the Islamic powers in the Levant and Egypt. The alliance is sealed by the marriage of Amalric's daughter Maria to Manuel's nephew John Komnenos. This alliance reflects the shared interests of the Crusader States and the Byzantine Empire in containing Islamic expansion and maintaining their territorial holdings in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: William of Tyre, History of the Crusades
Niketas Choniates, History of the Byzantine Empire

events.certainty_note: Both Crusader and Byzantine sources confirm the alliance between Amalric and Manuel I in 1165. The marriage of Maria and John Komnenos is well-attested.

events.title: Saladin consolidates power in Egypt under Nur ad-Din's authority
events.description: Following Shirkuh's death in 1166 (note: this event is misdated; Shirkuh died in 1166, not 1165), Saladin consolidates his position as the dominant military figure in Egypt. In 1165, Saladin is still subordinate to Nur ad-Din Zengi, the powerful Zengid ruler of Syria, but he is rapidly gaining influence and control over Egyptian affairs. Saladin's consolidation of power in Egypt during this period sets the stage for his later independence and rise to dominance in the Islamic world.
key_figures: Saladin (Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb, consolidating power)

sources.name: Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: Saladin's consolidation of power in Egypt occurred gradually over the 1160s. The specific dating of events to 1165 versus 1166 is uncertain; Shirkuh was still alive in 1165 and died in 1166.

events.title: Maimonides moves to Egypt
events.description: Moses Maimonides (Moshe ben Maimon), the renowned Jewish philosopher, physician, and legal scholar, moves to Egypt, settling in Cairo. Maimonides had previously lived in Al-Andalus and the Maghreb but now establishes himself in Egypt, where he will spend the rest of his life. In Egypt, Maimonides becomes a prominent physician and intellectual figure, eventually serving as the personal physician to Saladin. His move to Egypt marks the beginning of his most productive period, during which he will complete his major works.
key_figures: Moses Maimonides (Moshe ben Maimon, Jewish philosopher and physician)

sources.name: Maimonides' own writings and biographical sources

events.certainty_note: Maimonides moved to Egypt sometime in the 1160s. The exact year is uncertain; 1165 is a traditional date but may be off by a few years. Modern scholarship suggests the move occurred around 1165–1166.
cross_references: Part of broader Jewish intellectual flourishing in medieval Egypt

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Saladin's consolidation of power to 1165 is imprecise; Shirkuh was still alive in 1165 and died in 1166. Saladin's major consolidation occurred after Shirkuh's death in 1166. The dating of Maimonides' move to Egypt to 1165 is approximate; modern scholarship suggests the move occurred around 1165–1166, but the exact year is uncertain. The dating of Taira-Minamoto conflicts to 1165 is uncertain; the Heiji Rebellion occurred in 1159–1160, and subsequent conflicts are less precisely dated.
historiographic_note: The year 1165 is moderately well-documented in Islamic, European, and Japanese sources. Islamic chronicles provide detailed accounts of Nur ad-Din's death and its political consequences. European sources focus on Crusader military campaigns and alliances. Japanese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; cultural and intellectual developments are underrepresented except where they coincide with elite patronage. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia remain largely absent from surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Nur ad-Din's death in 1165 enabled Saladin's consolidation of power in Egypt
The Crusader-Byzantine alliance of 1165 enabled Amalric's invasion of Egypt in 1166
Maimonides' move to Egypt in 1165 enabled his completion of the Mishneh Torah in 1168
